meta.title: Expanded UMF benchmarks by glaze intent
meta.note: These are directional benchmark bands for recipe development and diagnosis. They are not approval limits and do not replace fit, durability, or firing tests.

profiles.name: Low-fire bright clear
profiles.surfaceGoal: very glossy transparent host for bright colour
chemistrySignals: clarity normally depends on a strong boron contribution
too much alumina quickly kills low-fire brilliance
this is often a colour-host chemistry, not a fit-maximized chemistry
watchFor: bright and clear does not mean low expansion
red bodies can still warm the final read through the clear

profiles.name: Low-fire lower-expansion clear
profiles.surfaceGoal: transparent with better fit on tighter bodies
chemistrySignals: 3249-style chemistry usually shows up somewhere in this direction
the lower-expansion branch often trades a little brilliance for survivability
moderate MgO helps fit but can flatten the surface if pushed too far
watchFor: if the clear looks dull, the problem may be under-melt or thickness, not only the lower expansion chemistry
low-fire fit still depends heavily on the body itself

profiles.name: Cone-6 stable clear liner
profiles.surfaceGoal: stable glossy transparent for liner or general host use
chemistrySignals: enough alumina to stay stable without turning cloudy
enough silica to behave like a liner rather than a fluid effect glaze
boron is present but not dominating the whole chemistry
watchFor: stable cone-6 clear is a chemistry and a thickness problem at the same time
using it as a universal host for every stain can still fail on sensitive warm colours

profiles.name: Cone-6 warm-colour host clear
profiles.surfaceGoal: glossy host that keeps chrome-tin and sensitive warm colours cleaner
chemistrySignals: the host should stay transparent and quiet rather than adding its own variegation
zinc-free or zinc-controlled direction is often safer for warm stain work
host choice matters as much as the colourant percentage
watchFor: adding opacifiers or titanium to the host often ruins the reason this chemistry was chosen
clean colour still needs a white or lighter body underneath

profiles.name: Cone-6 porcelain clear
profiles.surfaceGoal: glossy transparent with lower expansion for tight porcelain bodies
chemistrySignals: this family typically sacrifices some expansion to survive porcelain fit
a little MgO often helps pull the expansion down without becoming a matte
the chemistry still needs enough melt to stay truly clear
watchFor: a clear that fits porcelain can look needlessly tight or dull on a more open stoneware
do not assume one porcelain body represents all porcelain fit problems

profiles.name: Cone-6 silky functional matte
profiles.surfaceGoal: satin to silky matte with a better chance of functional performance
chemistrySignals: MgO should be present enough to soften the surface but not so high that the glaze goes waxy or dead
functional mattes usually need more chemistry discipline than decorative mattes
silky mattes often sit closer to glossy-clear chemistry than potters expect
watchFor: surface feel is not the same thing as durability
cooling can move the same recipe from satin to rough matte

profiles.name: Cone-6 calcium matte contrast
profiles.surfaceGoal: calcium-led matte or satin that contrasts with magnesia mattes
chemistrySignals: higher calcium can produce a cleaner, more mineral matte than a soft dolomite matte
small zinc participation can further redirect the surface
this route is useful as a comparison chemistry, not just as a production candidate
watchFor: colour response differs from MgO mattes even when the surface sheen looks similar
stain compatibility can shift when zinc is involved

profiles.name: Cone-6 reactive floating blue chemistry
profiles.surfaceGoal: blue pools, cream breaks, and floating variegation
chemistrySignals: this family is deliberately more fluid and reactive than a liner clear
lower alumina and active fluxes leave room for breaking and pooling
rutile, cobalt, and often tin are only part of the look; the host fluidity is the other part
watchFor: if the chemistry gets too stable the recipe stops floating and only stays blue
if the chemistry gets too fluid the result becomes a run test instead of a glaze

profiles.name: Cone-6 clean celadon direction
profiles.surfaceGoal: clean green celadon-like transparent with light iron
chemistrySignals: the host should stay transparent and restrained
iron is usually low enough to tint rather than saturate
body colour matters almost as much as the recipe here
watchFor: dark or iron-rich bodies can wipe out the clean celadon read
too much opacity or rutile turns the family into variegated green rather than celadon

profiles.name: Cone-10 gloss clear
chemistrySignals: high-fire gloss usually leans on calcium, feldspar, and silica more than on boron
the chemistry should look more finished and less forced than a low-fire clear
high silica only helps if the kiln actually delivers the heatwork
watchFor: cone-10 clarity can disguise poor fit if the body is not part of the test set
reduction and oxidation can both count as cone-10 but do not produce the same look

profiles.name: Cone-10 dolomite satin matte
profiles.surfaceGoal: silky reduction matte with enough melt to avoid a dead surface
chemistrySignals: MgO should be high enough to create softness without killing the melt
this chemistry often feels better than it looks in unity numbers alone
dolomite matte success still depends on firing, cooling, and body
watchFor: beautiful silky mattes can still be weak or mark-prone
legacy materials and substitutions can shift the feel faster than the formula suggests

profiles.name: Cone-10 iron gloss or temmoku direction
profiles.surfaceGoal: dark transparent iron gloss rather than opaque black
chemistrySignals: the host is still a transparent iron-bearing glass rather than an opaque stain base
iron loading and atmosphere do the colour work on top of a fundamentally glossy chemistry
too much stiffness kills depth and turns the result muddy
watchFor: iron saturation can make the glaze look black without actually producing a rich temmoku depth
a body that contributes too much iron can collapse the colour separation

diagnostics.issue: A cone-6 clear looks good but crazes on porcelain.
moveDirection: lower the K2O and Na2O pressure
move part of the flux package toward the lower-expansion clear family
keep enough silica and alumina to stay transparent

diagnostics.issue: A matte is dry, rough, or cutlery-marking badly.
moveDirection: move away from the most extreme MgO or under-melted zone
compare against the silky-functional matte profile
check cooling before rewriting the chemistry

diagnostics.issue: The blue is flat and even instead of breaking cream and blue.
moveDirection: compare against the reactive floating-blue benchmark instead of a liner clear
check whether alumina and overall stability are too high
verify thickness and texture response before increasing cobalt

diagnostics.issue: A celadon test drifts toward amber or brown.
moveDirection: reduce iron loading back toward the tint zone
use a cleaner transparent host and lighter body

diagnostics.issue: A low-fire clear is cloudy or milky without being intended as a milky clear.
moveDirection: avoid over-stiffening with alumina or clay
make sure the glaze actually matured at the intended heatwork

diagnostics.issue: A cone-10 transparent looks underfired or dry.
moveDirection: confirm witness-cone heatwork before changing chemistry
check whether silica and alumina outran the delivered heatwork
compare against the gloss-clear benchmark before adding extra flux blindly
